concepts.title: Recurrent networks — weights shared over time

beats.text: A recurrent network reads a sequence one step at a time and carries a hidden state forward.
At each step the cell updates its hidden state from the current input and the previous state, then emits an output.

visual.note: the same weights at every step

beats.text: The weights are tied across all T steps, so the cell reading token one is identical to the cell reading token one thousand.
Unrolling the loop reveals a feed-forward network of depth T whose layers happen to share parameters.
With Wxh equal to 2 and a zero start, the first hidden state is tanh of one, about 0.7616.
The output head is flexible, from many-to-one sentiment to many-to-many tagging to one-to-many generation.
Recurrence promises long-range memory, but a vanilla RNN cannot reach back hundreds of tokens to fill a blank.
The gradient of the final state with respect to a distant state is a long product of Jacobian factors.
Each factor is bounded by the recurrent weight norm, so the whole product behaves like a power of distance.

visual.note: geometric in the gap T minus k

beats.text: With an effective gain of 0.8, a gradient from fifty steps away is scaled by about fourteen millionths.
At gain 0.9, traveling forty-four steps scales the gradient to about 0.0097, roughly one percent of its size.
Exploding gradients are clipped cheaply, but vanishing ones are silent and need an architectural cure.
The LSTM adds a second memory track, the cell state, whose update is mostly additive rather than a repeated matrix multiply.
Three sigmoid gates act as soft valves that decide what to keep, what to add, and what to read out.
The cell scales old memory by the forget gate, scales the candidate by the input gate, then adds them.
When the forget gate is near one and the input gate near zero, the cell barely changes and the gradient flows back undecayed.
Counting the sigmoid valves, forget, input, and output, a standard LSTM has exactly three gates.
The forget bias usually starts at plus one, so the network defaults to remembering and learns to forget only when needed.
The GRU asks how much LSTM machinery is essential and answers with one state vector and two gates.
The update gate interpolates between keeping the old state and overwriting it with the candidate.

visual.note: one knob blends keep and write

beats.text: A single update gate does the work of the LSTM's separate forget and input gates, summing to one by construction.
The GRU keeps one state vector against the LSTM's two, and roughly three weight matrices against four.
On many tasks GRU and LSTM are statistically indistinguishable, and the gap is small and task-dependent.
What both share is the additive gated state path, and that idea resurfaces in modern state-space models like Mamba.
Training unrolls the network into its depth-T equivalent and runs ordinary backpropagation through it.
Because weights are shared, the gradient with respect to a weight is the sum of its contributions at every step.
The inner routing back through every earlier state uses the same Jacobian product that produces vanishing gradients.

visual.tex: \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial W} = \sum_{k=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial h_T}\, \frac{\partial h_T}{\partial h_k}\, \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W}

beats.text: Truncated BPTT cuts the sequence into chunks of about sixty-four to two hundred fifty-six steps.
It backpropagates only within a chunk but carries the hidden state forward, so the forward pass still sees unlimited context.
Three ideas form one thread: recurrence makes a deep net, depth makes a long product, and gates give it a highway.
